ground_truth.title: What is Git and How Does Version Control Work? | Terabyte Systems
ground_truth.author: David Kim
ground_truth.main_content: If you write code, you need to use Git. Full stop. It's become so fundamental to software development that it's hard to imagine working without it. But here's the thing: a lot of developers use Git without really understanding what's happening under the hood. I'm going to walk you through what Git actually does, why it's designed the way it is, and how to use it effectively. By the end, you'll understand not just the commands, but the underlying model that makes Git so powerful. Before we dive into Git specifically, let's talk about why version control exists at all. Git isn't the only version control system out there. Before Git became dominant, systems like SVN (Subversion) and CVS were common. With Git, every developer has a complete copy of the repository with full history. Here's where it gets interesting. Git doesn't store your files the way you might think. Understanding this model is key to mastering Git. Here's what blew my mind when I first learned it: Git doesn't store file changes (deltas), it stores snapshots. When you commit, Git takes a snapshot of all your files and stores a reference to that snapshot. If files haven't changed, Git doesn't store them again-it just links to the previous version. Branches in Git are incredibly lightweight-they're just pointers to commits. This is why branching is so fast in Git. I prefer git fetch plus git merge over git pull because it lets me review changes before merging them into my local branch. Git is one of those tools that seems simple on the surface but has incredible depth. The key to mastering it is understanding the underlying model: snapshots, not deltas; branches as pointers; commits as nodes in a graph. Once you internalize these concepts, Git's commands start to make sense. You stop memorizing recipes and start understanding what each command actually does to the repository.
with: If you write code, you need to use Git. Full stop. It's become so fundamental to software development that it's hard to imagine working without it.
Every file in your Git repository can be in one of three states: Modified, Staged, or Committed.
Git doesn't store file changes (deltas), it stores snapshots. When you commit, Git takes a snapshot of all your files and stores a reference to that snapshot.
Branches in Git are incredibly lightweight-they're just pointers to commits. This is why branching is so fast in Git.
The key to mastering it is understanding the underlying model: snapshots, not deltas; branches as pointers; commits as nodes in a graph.
